summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Microsoft's decision to ship Visual Studio 2005 (Whidbey) with an updated version of Visual SourceSafe rather than a lighter version of the new Team System. While acknowledging VSS will get some improvements like remote access and better performance, he argues the decade-old application is fundamentally broken and needs replacement, not incremental updates. He sees this as fragmenting the developer community into VSS and Team System users, creating a caste system where modern source control becomes an expensive enterprise-only feature. The decision treats source control—a fundamental development need—as an afterthought.
key_points: Visual SourceSafe is a ten-year-old application that needs replacement, not incremental improvements—basic features like branching are unusable
Shipping both VSS and Team System fragments developers into incompatible ecosystems, with VSS likely seeing wider adoption simply because it's included by default
Team System appears destined for the expensive enterprise market, leaving everyday developers with inferior tools
Source control is a fundamental development function that Microsoft is treating as a stopgap concern
The VS 2005 Express products don't support source control at all, compounding the problem
money_quotes: Visual SourceSafe sucks. I'm sorry, but it has to be said.
This isn't an app that needs some incremental improvements, it's an app that needs to be taken out behind the woodshed and put out of its misery.
It is acceptable for rudimentary 'better than nothing' source control, but not much more.
We can't even use branching on our projects at work because VSS has such terrible support for it!
It creates a development caste system.
How about a nice, modern version of source control for the common folk, without all that enterprise marketing crap (and the corresponding sticker shock).
Source control is a bread and butter function of development.
key_insight: Essential developer tools like source control should be modernized and democratized, not stratified into expensive enterprise products while legacy software gets life support.
